accepted.question_text: Determine what criteria decide whether a positive series sum a_n converges or diverges. Equivalently, is there a universal algorithmic convergence test, or any general structural pattern, that characterizes convergence versus divergence for arbitrary positive series?

evidence.quote: Problem 1. What decides if a positive series is convergent or divergent? Is there any universal convergence test? Is there any pattern in convergence?
Problem 2. Find an algorithm to determine whether a positive series is convergent or not.

accepted.question_text: Let A be a subset of the positive integers, and for an increasing function ϕ: N -> (0, ∞) with ϕ(n) -> ∞, define the weighted upper density by d̄_ϕ(A) = lim sup |A ∩ [1, n]| / ϕ(n). If d̄_{n/ln n}(A) > 0, must the harmonic subseries sum_{n in A} 1/n diverge? More generally, does the same conclusion hold for weights of the form n/[(ln n)(ln ln n)...(ln^(k) n)]?
accepted.context_brief: Weighted-density criteria for divergent harmonic subseries

evidence.quote: Problem 3. Given an increasing function ϕ : N →(0, ∞) with limn→∞ ϕ(n) = ∞, we define the upper density of weight ϕ by the formula
Does every subset A ⊂ N with d¯n/ ln n (A) > 0 generate a divergent subseries n∈A 1/n of the harmonic series? What about the case of other weights n/[(ln n) (ln ln n) · · · (ln^(k) n)]?

accepted.question_text: If A is a set of positive integers such that the reciprocal series sum_{n in A} 1/n diverges, must A contain arithmetic progressions of arbitrarily large finite length?
accepted.context_brief: Erdos conjecture on divergent reciprocal sets

evidence.quote: Conjecture 1. (P. Erdős). If the sum of reciprocals of a set A of integers diverges, then that set contains arbitrarily long arithmetic progressions. This conjecture is still open even if one only seeks a single progression of length three.
